stem: A manufacturing study measures defect rate across 4 factorys, each containing multiple production lines. factory A has 10 observations with mean 79.3 % (within-factory SD = 7.0). factory B has 8 observations with mean 44.1 % (within-factory SD = 7.3). factory C has 15 observations with mean 25.4 % (within-factory SD = 6.8). factory D has 5 observations with mean 70.8 % (within-factory SD = 8.0). The between-factory standard deviation is uncertain, estimated between 7.0 and 17.4 %. Using a hierarchical Bayesian model, estimate the true defect rate for factory B, accounting for shrinkage toward the grand mean and the uncertainty in the between-factory variation.

ground_truth.answer: Hierarchical estimate for factory B: 44.4 % (shrunk from raw 44.1). Range [44.2, 44.7] as between-factory SD varies from 7.0 to 17.4. Pooled mean: 49.5.

solution_steps: Compute pooled (grand) mean: 49.5 % (weighted average of 4 factory means).
Shrinkage factor for factory B: B = σ²_between / (σ²_between + σ²_within/8). At σ_between=11.6: B = 0.953.
Interval [44.2, 44.7] captures uncertainty from the unknown between-group variance.
common_failure_modes: Using the raw observed mean (44.1) without shrinkage
Using only the grand mean (49.5) and ignoring group-specific data
Not recognizing that small-sample groups should be shrunk more toward the grand mean
Treating the between-group variance as known rather than uncertain
